common_name: Dwarf Wax Myrtle (Dwarf Southern Wax Myrtle, Compact Wax Myrtle)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf wax myrtle is extremely tolerant of salt spray, drought, wet soils, and poor soils, making it one of the most adaptable native shrubs for Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf wax myrtle is a compact native shrub closely related to southern wax myrtle (Morella cerifera) but naturally staying much smaller — 2–4 feet tall and wide — without requiring the heavy shearing that makes the larger species high-maintenance. The aromatic, semi-evergreen to evergreen, dark-green, spatula-shaped leaves have the same pleasant, bay-like fragrance when crushed as its larger relative. Small clusters of waxy, steel-blue berries form on female plants in fall and winter, providing essential high-fat food for dozens of native bird species — a cornerstone plant for wildlife habitat. Dwarf wax myrtle is extraordinarily adaptable: it tolerates salt spray, poor sandy soil, clay, periodic flooding, and significant drought once established, making it one of the most valuable natives for difficult Gulf Coast sites. Excellent as a naturalized hedge, mass planting, or foundation shrub where its compact size fits without constant pruning.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Florida Native Landscaping

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; rough-leaf hydrangea blooms on old wood — avoid pruning in fall or spring, trimming only immediately after flowering if needed.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; rough-leaf hydrangea is a large, bold-textured shrub from Asia that differs dramatically from the common mophead and lacecap hydrangeas — it produces enormous, velvety, felted leaves up to 10 inches long on stout stems covered with coarse hairs, giving the plant a distinctly tropical, textural quality. The large lacecap flower heads in midsummer bear pale lavender to purple fertile flowers surrounded by white to soft pink sterile ray flowers in a classic lacecap pattern, but on a much larger scale than Hydrangea macrophylla. The combination of the huge, rough-textured foliage and the lacecap flowers makes it an outstanding architectural shrub for woodland shade gardens. It performs better than mophead hydrangeas in zone 7–9 heat and humidity. The subspecies and cultivar 'Macrophylla' (also called villosa hydrangea) is the most widely grown selection. Provide consistent moisture and protection from drying winds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; First Frost is a sport of Halcyon hosta and shares its parent's notably good heat tolerance among blue-leafed hostas.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; First Frost hosta is a sport of the much-loved 'Halcyon' cultivar, maintaining the parent's clear blue-gray leaf color while adding a broad, creamy-white to yellow margin that brightens and lightens in the shade garden. Named the American Hosta Growers Association Hosta of the Year in 2010, it represents an outstanding example of selecting from sports — the natural mutations that appear spontaneously on existing cultivars. The leaves are medium-sized, broadly ovate, and held more upright than many hostas, creating an attractive vase-like silhouette. Light lavender flowers appear on tall scapes in midsummer. Like Halcyon, First Frost has better heat tolerance than most blue hostas and performs reasonably well in zone 8–9 when given consistent moisture and deep shade. The blue-gray center contrasting with the cream-to-yellow margin creates a sophisticated two-tone effect that combines beautifully with chartreuse-leaved companions.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Night Before Christmas has wide white centers that intensify in light shade — deep shade may cause the white to become somewhat translucent and less vivid.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Night Before Christmas is a medium hosta producing striking foliage with the color pattern reversed from most variegated hostas — a broad, bright white center surrounded by a dark green margin rather than the more common green center with lighter margin. The wide white center areas create exceptional brightness in dark shaded locations, almost appearing luminous in deep shade, making this one of the most effective hostas for illuminating dark corners of the garden. The white portion may show some sun scorch if exposed to afternoon sun, so full shade or dappled light is best. Near-white flowers appear on tall scapes in midsummer. Night Before Christmas is a steady-growing medium cultivar that reaches mature size in 3–4 years and forms a well-mannered clump without the rapid spreading tendencies of some smaller hostas. The bold, unusual reversed variegation makes it a prized addition to the serious hosta collection.

common_name: Fall Muhly (Purple Muhly, Deer Grass Muhly)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; fall muhly is drought-adapted and performs best in lean soils without supplemental irrigation once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; fall muhly is a western and southwestern native grass closely related to Gulf muhly and pink muhly grass, producing somewhat coarser, denser clumps of deep-green foliage and vivid purple, airy seed plumes in fall — the purple color is more intense and held longer than some other muhly species. It is an outstanding grass for hot, dry, rocky garden conditions in zone 7–10, thriving in the rocky limestone and caliche soils of central Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona where many ornamental grasses struggle. The fall color display — masses of burgundy-purple plumes catching the fall light — is one of the most beautiful seasonal displays available in a drought-tolerant native grass. Excellent for xeriscape, parking lot medians, rocky slopes, and any location that receives reflected heat from pavement. Cut back to 6–8 inches in late winter before new growth begins. Combines beautifully with fall-blooming natives like asters, salvias, and fall-blooming sedums.

common_name: Dwarf Yaupon Holly (Schilling's Dwarf Yaupon, Nana Yaupon)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in any well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf yaupon is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for the Gulf Coast, tolerating everything from salt spray to drought to poorly drained clay soils.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf yaupon holly is a compact, mounding cultivar of the native yaupon holly that stays 2–3 feet tall and 3–5 feet wide without requiring the constant shearing that maintains the rounded shape of unselected yaupon. The 'Nana' and 'Schillings' cultivars are the most widely grown dwarf forms, producing the characteristic small, slightly toothed, dark-green leaves of the species in a naturally dense, low, spreading mound that functions beautifully as a foundation plant, hedge, or mass planting. Being male cultivars, these dwarf selections do not produce berries — for berry production, plant named female selections like 'Hoskins Shadow' or unselected yaupon alongside. Dwarf yaupon is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once established, enduring the Gulf Coast's extreme summer conditions without supplemental irrigation in established plantings. The deer resistance and extreme adaptability make it one of the most reliable low-maintenance native shrubs for zone 7–10.
